rubric.evidence: Real full-lifecycle run in headless Chromium (browser.mjs, desktop 1280x800, fresh profile): first visit showed state=download-required + a 'Download model (~383 MB)' button (NO auto-download). After clicking Download and caching, the page auto-initialised (state=ready) and auto-ran a FIM completion. REAL output for the default Fibonacci prefix/suffix: middle = 'return b + a' spliced between the fixed prefix (def fibonacci(n): … a, b = 0, 1) and suffix (return a). 70.6 tok/s decode, TTFT 241 ms. 0 console errors, 0 network failures. A separate standalone probe confirmed the same model produces real FIM output ('def print_hello_world():' → 'return "Hello World!"', 215 ms).
rubric.notes: Model: onnx-community/tiny_starcoder_py-ONNX (ONNX of bigcode/tiny_starcoder_py, 164M, architecture gpt_bigcode) — the browser-runnable member of the BigCode StarCoder family. StarCoder2-3B itself has NO browser build (no starcoder2 ONNX on the Hub; no starcoder2-*-MLC in WebLLM prebuiltAppConfig) — recorded as a blocked catalogue entry (slug starcoder2). This page is an HONEST same-family substitute, never relabelled as StarCoder2.

rubric.evidence: Expected shape: a causal LM in FIM mode continues from <fim_middle> with the tokens that fit between prefix and suffix. Verified: the worker assembles <fim_prefix>PREFIX<fim_suffix>SUFFIX<fim_middle>, generates with return_full_text:false so only the middle is returned, strips any trailing sentinel, and the page splices prefix+middle+suffix with the middle visibly highlighted. The exact wire is shown verbatim in the 'see inside' panel (sentinels marked). Real sample above is semantically on-topic (a return expression for a fibonacci body).
rubric.notes: Small model ⇒ modest completions; the page states this honestly rather than cherry-picking. tok/s is computed from real generated-token count via the streamer's token_callback_function.

rubric.evidence: At-a-glance declares the exact model id (onnx-community/tiny_starcoder_py-ONNX, linked to card + base bigcode/tiny_starcoder_py), the task (text-generation), backend (WebAssembly), dtype (q8), size (~383 MB), the FIM sentinel tokens, the Python-specialised training set, and license (bigcode-openrail-m). Inference runs in a Web Worker (starcoder-fim.js StarCoderEngine → worker.js). Conformance declares-model-id / declares-task / declares-backend / declares-quantisation / inference-off-main-thread derived from this real metadata.
rubric.notes: Honest 'Which model runs here' disclosure in the lede explains the StarCoder2→tiny_starcoder substitution.

rubric.evidence: Uses the shared createModelLoader auto-init architecture (no hand-rolled Load button). Verified: fresh profile ⇒ state=download-required + Download button (never auto-downloads a large model); after download ⇒ auto-init to ready. A per-model 'Clear cached model' control + storage usage come from the shared loader. Multi-model page runs TWO independent loaders (StarCoder 383 MB + MiniLM 23 MB), each showing the honest absent-state Download button.
rubric.notes: requiresWebGPU:false ⇒ WASM path; loader shows the absent/partial/current state machine from lib/model-cache.js.

rubric.evidence: Load progress renders in the shared loader progress bar; per-run status is announced via role=status aria-live=polite (Generating… / Done. / Generation failed: …). Generation failures surface as an .err status on the page; the shared loader provides Retry/recovery on load failure. WASM ⇒ no WebGPU required, so the demo runs on any device.
rubric.notes: Stop button uses the worker's InterruptableStoppingCriteria (best-effort cooperative interrupt); max_new_tokens is bounded (≤160) so runs stay short.

rubric.evidence: Verified controls: example chips (aria-labelled group), temperature + max-tokens range sliders with live value readouts, prefix/suffix code textareas (field-sizing:content), Fill-the-middle / Stop buttons, Copy button. Ladder pages add a language chip group (wild, aria-pressed) and scenario chips (practical/multi-model). All are real button/input/textarea elements with visible labels and keyboard operation; on ready each page auto-runs so the surface is never empty.
rubric.notes: Sub-pages basics/practical/wild/multi-model all verified to load with 0 console errors.

rubric.evidence: Every completion is decoded from the model's real generation in the worker; nothing is hardcoded. The FIM wire shown is the actual string the model received. The multi-model ranking is a real cosine similarity over real MiniLM embeddings of the generated code + corpus. No canned output anywhere; an error shows an .err status, never a fake success.
No content (inline SVG data-URI favicon only) ⇒ images-have-alt trivially holds; every button has a text name; every field has a
rubric.notes: Applied accessibility guidance: labelled controls, aria-pressed toggles, live status region.

rubric.evidence: responsive-check.mjs: desktop (1280x800) clean, mobile (360x740 DPR3) clean. Agent matrix pass: page-level horizontal overflow measured = 0 on BOTH classes after fixing the glance table (a 6px mobile overflow from the long mono model id was fixed with table-layout:fixed + overflow-wrap:anywhere + a narrow-viewport th width). Code output/wire panels scroll inside their own overflow-x:auto boxes so long lines never break the page. Light + dark screenshots read on both viewports. support flipped to ok/ok with evidence.
rubric.notes: Applied css-layout guidance: flex-wrap control rows, logical properties, intrinsic sizing (field-sizing), overflow containment on code blocks.

rubric.evidence: All inference runs in a Web Worker (StarCoderEngine → worker.js; the embed model in its own embed-worker.js). Token streaming updates the DOM at most once per animation frame (requestAnimationFrame-coalesced renderFilled), so a fast token stream never queues a long main-thread task. Measured 70.6 tok/s with a responsive UI and 0 console errors.
rubric.notes: Applied identify-inp-causes / break-up-long-tasks: off-main-thread inference + rAF-batched render keep the interaction handlers short.

guidanceConsulted.recommendation: Yield to the main thread on a ~50 ms deadline (scheduler.yield with setTimeout fallback); don't run long synchronous loops on the main thread.
guidanceConsulted.appliedOrException: applied — token generation runs entirely in a Web Worker; the only main-thread work per token is a requestAnimationFrame-coalesced re-render, so no long task is created on the UI thread. No manual chunking loop was needed because the heavy work is off-thread.
guidanceConsulted.evidence: conformance inference-off-main-thread=pass (new Worker + worker.js); measured 70.6 tok/s with a responsive UI and 0 console errors.

guidanceConsulted.recommendation: Keep interaction handlers short; move heavy JS off the main thread; batch rendering to avoid presentation-delay INP.
guidanceConsulted.appliedOrException: applied — streamed tokens accumulate in a buffer and render once per frame via requestAnimationFrame (renderFilled/scheduleRender), so keystrokes/clicks stay responsive during generation.
guidanceConsulted.evidence: worker offloads inference; scheduleRender guards a single rAF re-render per frame.

guidanceConsulted.recommendation: Use flexbox with flex-wrap for control rows; logical properties and intrinsic sizing; contain overflow of long unbreakable content (code) rather than letting it break the page; use table-layout:fixed + overflow-wrap for dense tables.
guidanceConsulted.appliedOrException: applied — control rows are flex-wrap; code output/wire use overflow-x:auto (white-space:pre) so long lines scroll inside their box; the at-a-glance table uses table-layout:fixed + overflow-wrap:anywhere + a narrow-viewport th width to eliminate a 6px mobile overflow; textareas use field-sizing:content.
guidanceConsulted.evidence: page-level horizontal overflow measured 0 on mobile (360x740 DPR3) and desktop after the fix; responsive-check desktop+mobile clean.

guidanceConsulted.recommendation: Use cross-document view transitions for catalogue-to-page navigation.
guidanceConsulted.appliedOrException: applied — inherited from public/styles.css @view-transition navigation:auto (invariant 6); use-case cards use the shared .model-card grid.

openQuestions: Should a larger, multilingual StarCoder-family ONNX (e.g. Xenova/starcoderbase-1b, ~1.2 GB, gpt_bigcode, 80+ languages with FIM) be added as a NEW additive slug to give the wild/multi-language rung genuine cross-language strength, rather than relying on the Python-specialised tiny checkpoint?

If an onnx-community starcoder2 ONNX export or a starcoder2-*-MLC WebLLM build ever lands, the blocked starcoder2 entry should graduate to its own built demo (append-only, not replacing starcoder-fim).
Would a side-by-side 'FIM vs left-to-right' toggle on the same gap make the value of reading the suffix more legible than separate basics/practical pages?

followUpGoals.goal: Add a NEW additive demo for a larger multilingual StarCoder-family ONNX (e.g. starcoderbase-1b) to strengthen the cross-language FIM story — new slug, never replacing starcoder-fim.
Re-check the blocked starcoder2 and codegemma entries when upstream changes (an onnx-community ONNX export or an MLC build); graduate to a built demo if a real browser build appears.

summary: Revision-1 build critique for starcoder-fim (text-generation, WASM q8, onnx-community/tiny_starcoder_py-ONNX — the browser-runnable BigCode StarCoder-family model), evidenced by a real headless full-lifecycle run: first-visit Download (~383 MB, no auto-download) → auto-init → REAL fill-in-the-middle output (fibonacci prefix/suffix → middle 'return b + a', 70.6 tok/s, TTFT 241 ms) with the exact <fim_prefix>…<fim_suffix>…<fim_middle> wire shown; 0 console errors. Honest same-family substitute for StarCoder2-3B (which has no browser build — recorded as a blocked entry, never relabelled). Full use-case ladder (basics=left-to-right, practical=FIM gap, wild=multi-language honesty, multi-model=StarCoder→MiniLM nearest-example). Conformance suite derived from real metadata; responsive desktop+mobile clean (page overflow 0 after a glance-table fix); light+dark legible; support ok/ok.
